reason:
Both answers correctly identify and elaborate on all four key points: reframing the comparison axis, weaponizing the incumbent's complexity/cost as a liability, targeting buyers who don't care about the checklist, and getting ahead of the RFP/eval criteria rather than entering a bake-off already lost. Structurally near-identical (5-6 numbered moves plus a closing synthesis line), with no factual errors in either.

Differences are minor and roughly offsetting: Answer A adds a distinct tactic ('concede honestly' — naming where the incumbent wins to build trust and disqualify bad-fit deals early) and a concrete content-marketing suggestion (a 'why teams switch' page). Answer B adds a slightly sharper framing on forcing your metric into the bake-off itself (publish/demo live, insist the eval measure it) and calls out bottoms-up PLG tactics (docs, DX, free tier) for winning the underserved buyer. Neither addition is decisive enough to change what the asker would do next in a way the other answer doesn't already substantially cover.

Given equivalent correctness, coverage of the key points, and comparable actionable specificity, with each having one small unique tactical nugget that roughly cancels the other's, this is a genuine tie.
